coredata.dc:title: Dual-emissive waste oil based S-doped carbon dots for acetone detection and Cr(VI) detection/reduction/removal
coredata.dc:creator: Rangel, Miriam
Saluja, Sarvagya
Barba, V.
Pérez-Huerta, J.S.
Agarwal, V.
coredata.dc:description: Dual-emissive water-dispersible sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) were prepared through simple heat treatment of canola oil as a carbon source. The S-CDs revealed excitation wavelength-dependent and independent emission band in UV (327 nm) and the visible region (633 nm), respectively. The fluorescent S-CDs ranged from 4 to 9 nm in size and exhibited selectivity and linear “turn-off” fluorescence in the presence of Cr(VI) and acetone, making them potential nanosensors for their detection. Concentration-dependent red-shift of the S-CDs emission spectrum was observed in the presence of acetone, via incremental quenching of the peaks at 327 nm and 633 nm, accompanied by an emergence of new peak at adjacent higher wavelength. Linearity was observed in the range of 0.01–0.08 vol%, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.01 vol%. The nanoprobe was tested in spiked tap and river water for Cr(VI) along with acetone detection assays in spiked human urine and phosphate buffer solution. XPS analysis revealed the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) in the presence of S-CDs, a less toxic form. In addition to the optical detection, fluorescent probes incorporated into poly vinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix could be used for the removal of Cr(VI) ions from the contaminated water. Hence, the proposed S-CDs have potential applications as highly sensitive, selective, cost-effective, and environmental friendly sensors of acetone and Cr(VI), as well as for developing tools for the reduction and removal of Cr(VI) from contaminated water.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual-emissive water-dispersible sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) were prepared through simple heat treatment of canola oil as a carbon source. The S-CDs revealed excitation wavelength-dependent and independent emission band in UV (327 nm) and the visible region (633 nm), respectively. The fluorescent S-CDs ranged from 4 to 9 nm in size and exhibited selectivity and linear “turn-off” fluorescence in the presence of Cr(VI) and acetone, making them potential nanosensors for their detection. Concentration-dependent red-shift of the S-CDs emission spectrum was observed in the presence of acetone, via incremental quenching of the peaks at 327 nm and 633 nm, accompanied by an emergence of new peak at adjacent higher wavelength. Linearity was observed in the range of 0.01–0.08 vol%, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.01 vol%. The nanoprobe was tested in spiked tap and river water for Cr(VI) along with acetone detection assays in spiked human urine and phosphate buffer solution. XPS analysis revealed the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) in the presence of S-CDs, a less toxic form. In addition to the optical detection, fluorescent probes incorporated into poly vinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix could be used for the removal of Cr(VI) ions from the contaminated water. Hence, the proposed S-CDs have potential applications as highly sensitive, selective, cost-effective, and environmental friendly sensors of acetone and Cr(VI), as well as for developing tools for the reduction and removal of Cr(VI) from contaminated water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid increase in population has resulted in a severe potable water scarcity, leading to an urgent need for wastewater remediation. Contamination due to human activities and industrial waste has left highly toxic organic dyes, heavy metals, and other pollutants in the environment. One of these is acetone, a hazardous organic solvent that is very miscible in water. Its presence in water could imply health affectations [1]. Apart from environmental remediation, the detection of ketones generated by the human body, such as acetone, is very important for individuals with diabetes or ketoacidosis. Trace amounts of acetone are typically detected using blood, urine or breath samples [1,3]. On the other hand, metallic pollutants, including heavy metals, in water resources are also a great challenge due to their adverse impact on health and the environment even in trace amounts. These inorganic, non-biodegradable pollutants are mainly present as cations and anions (Pb2+, Sn2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Cr2O7 2-, AsO4 3-, SeO3 2-, etc.). Although some of these metals are necessary for biological functions in small amounts, they can be dangerous in larger quantities, and can cause devastating damage to organs [4]. The seventh most abundant element on earth, chromium is present naturally and released anthropogenically in several oxidation states ranging from Cr(II) to Cr(VI). It is used extensively in paint production, metallurgy, paper, and chemical production, and in presence of excess environmental oxygen, oxidizes to the extremely toxic and highly water-soluble oxidation state Cr(VI). The resulting chromium greatly impacts the biological processes of plants and is categorized as a group 1 human carcinogen by the International Agency for the research on cancer due to its mutagenic properties [5]. Despite its dangerous nature, it has been witnessed at 2000 times the permissible limit in the underground water of Tokyo and at more than 12 mg/L in India [4]. So far, techniques such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), mass spectrometry, electrophoresis, electrochemical methods, and more have been used for the detection of environmental pollutants [6], [7]. However, these methods can be time-consuming, expensive, and use even more toxic solvents during analysis, drawing attention towards the relatively fast, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly fluorescence-based detection processes. Thus, detecting and monitoring heavy metals such as Cr(VI) and compounds like acetone in aqueous environments continues to be of great interest.

In recent years, the use of Carbon Dots (CDs) as optical sensors for environmental pollutants has attracted extensive attention due to their easily tunable fluorescent properties, uncomplicated synthesis, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, high chemical stability, water solubility, and low cost of production [3,8–10].

CDs can also be incorporated into transparent polymer matrices, which brings advantages such as the protection of CDs from concentration-induced PL self-quenching, improvement of their luminescent properties and further extended applications in fields of sensors, photovoltaics, photocatalysis, and others [11], [12]. There are two main ways of adding CDs into polymer matrices: physical dispersion or chemical polymerization [13]. Through physical interactions, the polymer conjugates with functional groups on the CDs surface [14]. In this work, we use waste canola oil (a popular cooking oil), to synthesize sulfur doped carbon dots as fluorescent probes for aqueous Cr(VI) and acetone. Waste cooking oil, is a major environmental pollutant where billions of gallons are generated every year by households and the food industry [15]. Unfortunately, its improper disposal is ubiquitous, causing it to end up in the environment and pollute soil, contaminate ground water, and severely harm surrounding plant, aquatic and human life [16], [17]. Canola oil contains oleic and - linoleic acids (around 65% and 12% respectively [18]); CDs were synthetized by heating the oil and doping with sulfuric acid, leading to multiple reaction pathways as dehydration, oxidation and polymer crosslinking to generate luminescent CDs [19], [20]. The resulting S-CDs can be used as fluorescent nanoprobes for the selective detection of Cr(VI) based on a photoluminescence “turn-off” mechanism. It also provides a “dual detection” mechanism for acetone based the two phenomena of PL quenching of the original emission wavelengths and the almost simultaneous emergence of red-shifted emission bands as the concentration of acetone increases. Moreover, the dual band emission property of the synthesized S-CDs provides great opportunities in fluorescence sensing applications [21]. The Cr(VI) quenching mechanism was attributed to the inner filter effect, along with its reduction to Cr(III). The applicability of S-CDs nanoprobes was demonstrated in Cr(VI) detection in tap and river water and acetone detection in artificial urine and phosphate buffer solution. S-CDs can also be incorporated into a PVA polymer film which allows the removal of chromium in water due to its porous and fibrous morphology which is able to adsorb ions interacting with the S-CDs.

S-CDs were prepared via carbonization [22] with some modifications. Briefly, S-CDs were obtained by heating as packaged and used/residual canola oil (during frying it was heated to 200ºC for 2 h) of a commercial brand (5 mL) in a pre-heated oil bath at 100 ºC for 25 min, followed by the slow addition of 1 mL of sulfuric acid (97.4%). The mixture was stirred continuously for another 5 min till it reached a temperature of 160 ºC. The mixture was cooled, then diluted with 25 mL of deionized water and neutralized with a 3 M NaOH solution. The produced yellowish suspension was subjected to syringe filtration (0.22 µm membrane) to remove larger carbon residues. Furthermore, the suspension was also centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 20 min, removing the oil part over the supernatant. These procedures resulted in clear yellowish solution of S-CDs which were then characterized. The use of either fresh or waste canola oil as carbon source resulted in CDs with an extremely similar emission spectrum, suggesting that even waste oil can be used to create similar CDs (Fig. S1).

S-CDs/PVA films with varying S-CD content were made. 1 g PVA was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water and stirred at 90 ºC during 3 h, after which 3 mL of only PVA solution was mixed with 5%, 10% and 20% vol of the S-CD suspension. The mixture was stirred for 1 h more at 60 ºC, poured into a glass petri dish (diameter of 5 cm) and stored for 24 h maintaining 60 ºC to obtain white films.

Standard Canola oil was purchased from local market. SnCl2, AgNO3, FeSO4, CoCl2, HgCl2, NiSO4, CuCl2, K2Cr2O7, ZnCl2, Mg(NO3)2, KCl, NaCl, Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, urea, phosphate buffer pH 7.2, PVA (Mw 31,000–50,000) and 1, 5- diphenylcarbazide (DPC) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. H2SO4 (97.4%) was acquired from Fermont. Deionized water was employed in all experiments unless otherwise noted.

Absorbance was measured in a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950 spectrophotometer and PL spectra were obtained using a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. The FTIR spectra were acquired in a Varian 660-IR FT-IR spectrophotometer. S-CDs morphology and particle size distribution were analyzed in a TEM-JEOL JEM-2010. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were attained with a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer using monochromatized CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). XPS spectra were obtained in a Thermo scientific Escalab 250Xi equipment. Zeta-potential and DLS measurements were recorded using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano series equipment. Finally, the FE-SEM (Hitachi S5500) was used to confirm the morphology of the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay for sensing Cr(VI) and acetone
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of Cr(VI) and acetone was performed at room temperature in an aqueous solution. A control sample of the S-CDs was prepared by adding 5 µL of S-CDs to 250 µL of water. To perform metal ion detection, 20 µL of S-CDs stock solution was added to 250 µL of metallic ion solution (made in deionized water), and the PL intensity was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 260 nm and a constant excitation/emission slit width of 10 nm. Selectivity was determined using 10 mM metallic solutions. In the case of solvents, 20 µL of S-CDs were dispersed in 250 µL of different solvents in pure form. Sensitivity experiment were carried out by adding 5 µL of S-CDs to 250 µL of metallic and acetone solutions at different concentrations. To detect acetone, synthetic urine was prepared by mixing 0.01 g of KCl, 4 g of NaCl, 0.57 g of Na2HPO4, 0.1 g of KH2PO4, 4.6 g of urea in 500 mL of deionized water (pH was adjusted to 8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stern Volmer plots and determination of limit of detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The experimental PL data were fitted using the Stern Volmer equation [23, 24]:

where
F

is the PL intensity of S-CDs in water, F is the PL intensity in the presence of the contaminant,
K
SV
is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant (slope of the concentration dependent response), and Q is the quencher concentration. Then, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the formula 3σ/K (σ is standard deviation and K is the slope of the calibration plot) [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric determination of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) reduction
ce:section.ce:para:
K2Cr2O7 was dried at 100 ºC, 2 h, then 141.4 mg were dissolved in 100 mL of ultra-pure water to obtain a concentration of 500 ppm. The stock solution was diluted to 50 ppm. Then 250 mg of DPC were aggregated to 50 mL of ethanol and sonicated 30 min. A solution of H2SO4 10% (v/v) was prepared. Reference consists of chromium 50 ppm and sample includes 20% (v/v) of S-CDs stock solution in 50 ppm of chromium. Both samples were measured at initial time and then were maintained under sunlight during 5 h. To measure the absorbance spectra, 200
μ L
of sample were collected and diluted to 3 mL with ultra-pure water and pH was regulated to 2 using H2SO4 10% (v/v), followed by the addition of 1 mL of DPC solution [26].

The UV–vis spectrum of the S-CDs ( Fig. 1a) reveals an absorption peak around 260 nm which is attributed to the π-π * energy transition of CC bonds in an aromatic core of the CDs. A weak absorption band observed around 330 nm is, most likely due to the n-π * transition of CO bonds [27].

The PL spectra of the S-CDs reveals the strongest emission peak at 327 nm when excited at a wavelength of 260 nm. A double-peak (327 and 633 nm) and excitation dependent photoluminescence signal is observed (Fig. 1b and c). When the excitation wavelength is increased within the range of 250–420 nm, the leftmost emission peak shifts from 327 to 492 nm, while the second peak shifts from 633 to 759 nm. The double peak can be attributed to two types of luminescence centers on the S-CDs i.e. the carbon core and the surface state [28, 29]. The relative PL quantum yield (QY) was calculated according to Eq. (S1) and it was determined to be 2.9%. Although broad PL and PLE spectra reveal the wide distribution in the dimensions in S-CDs and in turn the corresponding band gaps, the estimation of the direct optical band gap (E
g ) from the Tauc plot is ∼3.5 eV [30] (Fig. S1b).

TEM images of the S-CDs show rounded shapes with a size distribution of 4–8.8 nm in diameter ( Fig. 2a). Some lattice fringes (parallel lines over round shaped S-CDs) with a spacing of 0.21 nm, correspond to (100) plane of graphite lattice [31]. Lines with a spacing of 0.32 nm are also observed, which relates to graphitic interlayer spacing (Fig. 2b) [32]. In Fig. 2c DLS analysis of the S-CDs shows a size distribution range of 6–15 nm with a mean of 9.7 nm.

In order to identify the backbone and functional groups around S-CDs, FTIR spectrum was recorded (Fig. S2a). The spectrum displays vibrational bands of non-hydrogen-bonded O-H at 3648 cm−1, C-H stretching from methoxy groups at 2853 cm−1, and C-H stretch from methylene group at 2923 cm−1. It also shows vibrational bands corresponding to CO at 1697 cm−1, CC at 1650 cm−1, vinyl C-H in-plane bending vibrations at 1410 cm−1, SO at 1095 cm−1, C-S at 1045 cm−1 [33], S-O at 877 cm−1 [34], and C-S at 667 cm−1 [29]. The pair of bands at 1084 and 667 cm−1 indicate the presence of sulfate (or sulfonate) ions [35], which is in conformity with the proposal of a sulfur-functionalized surface on the CDs shown by Y. Hu and coworkers [22].

The XPS survey spectrum was recorded to confirm the presence of functional groups in S-CDs. The spectrum shows the signals corresponding to C1s, O1s and two peaks of sulfur (doped CDs), S2p and S2s (Fig. S2b). The atomic weight of composition is 50.38% of C, 38.46% of O and 11.16% of S. The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) was deconvoluted into four well resolved peaks located at 284.6 eV (C-C) [36], 286.2 eV (C-O) [37], 287.2 eV (C-O-C, C-S) [38], 288.9 eV (O-CO) [36]. For O1s spectrum (Fig. 2e), three main peaks were detected 531.7 (CO), 533.1 (C-O) and 536 eV (OC-OH) [22], [39]. Finally, the S2p spectrum (Fig. 2f) shows one well defined peak around 168.5 eV (S2p1/2) which confirm the sulfur doping in CDs in the form of sulfur oxide [38]. This peak could be deconvoluted into three peaks 167.6, 168.5 and 169.6 eV attributed to C-SOx (x = 2,3,4) [39].

The XRD patterns for S-CDs reveal a peak at 23.2º which corresponds to the plane (222) with and interlayer spacing of 0.38 nm and FWHM= 0.355 (Fig. S2c). The increased value of spacing compared to the value in bulk graphite (0.34 nm), could be due to abundant functional groups [22]. A peak at 23.5º [40] or 23.6º [22] has been reported in XRD patterns for sulfur doped CDs.

The zeta-potential plot of the S-CDs showed a single peak at − 39.7 mV (Fig. S3a), indicating that the S-CDs have net negative charge from the surface functional groups which allows them to become highly dispersed in water-based solvent. The relatively high zeta-potential absolute value also indicates long-term stability, in which particles will not tend to aggregate over time [41]. After six months of storage, the zeta-potential was found to be − 37.2 mV, confirming the predicted stability (Fig. S3b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability studies with NaCl, pH and time
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the PL signal stability of S-CDs, emission intensity was recorded at different NaCl concentration (Fig. S4a, and d) and pH (Fig. S4b, and e). The effect of aging on the optical properties of S-CDs was also monitored along the time period of six months of synthesis, demonstrating to be highly stable (Fig. S4c and f). The S-CDs showed further stability in the presence of NaCl, with PL emissions decreasing only 7% at 327 nm and 11% at 633 nm band, when NaCl concentration was increased from 0 to 3 M; while the PL intensity of the S-CDs showed very little variation with changes in pH, decreasing only 13% at 327 nm and 14% at 633 nm in a pH range from 1 to 14.

The relationship between temperature and PL was found to be linear, with a steady decrease in peak intensity for both peaks. Furthermore, the temperature response of PL intensity is fully reversible (Fig. S5a and b). The PL intensity decreases when the temperature is increased to 70 °C and reverts to its original value when the temperature is brought back down to 25 °C, throughout several cycles. The linear response reveals the potential application of such nanoprobes as reversible and reusable temperature sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity towards heavy metal ions and solvents
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the fluorescence response of the S-CDs in the presence of different metal ions (10 mM), their fluorescence response was measured under 260 nm excitation. Fig. 3 reveals complete quenching for both emission bands (327 and 633 nm) in the presence of Cr(VI), indicating high selectivity towards this ion and potential applications of the developed S-CDs in Cr(VI) heavy metal detection.

Fluorescence emission in the presence of different solvents was studied by suspending S-CDs in a solution of the pure solvent and measuring emission during excitation at 260 nm. As shown in Fig. 3, acetone considerably changes the maximum wavelength and intensity of the S-CD emission bands; in pure acetone, new and relatively smaller emission bands have emerged at 410 nm and 726 nm.

To study the sensitivity of S-CDs towards Cr(VI), 5 µL of S-CD suspension was mixed with 250 µL solutions containing Cr(VI) ions from 10 nM to 1 mM. Fig. 4a shows a continuous decrease in the PL intensity as Cr(VI) is increased, with a small shift in the position of the first band from 327 to 317 nm, and Fig. 4b shows the change in the second half of the recorded emission spectrum, with a steady decrease in band intensity at 633 nm. In Fig. 4c it is possible to see two useful linear relationships between the concentration of Cr(VI) and the change in PL intensity (F0/F) at low Cr(VI) concentrations (from 10 to 80 nM (Fig. S6A)). At 327 nm the relationship has a coefficient of determination (R2) = 0.96, F0/F= 1.2e-3[Cr(VI)] + 1.05, and LOD of 20 nM, while the intensity at 633 nm has an R2 of 0.94, F0/F= 1.1e-3 [Cr(VI)] + 1.08, and LOD of 20 nM (Fig. S6a) which is lower than the permissible limits of Cr(VI), in drinking water, according to the World Health Organization ∼0.9 μM [42]. In addition, interference in the quenching of S-CDs by Cr(VI) due to the presence of other metal ions (at 2 mM) was studied. Results revealed no significant interference (Fig. S6b), further establishing a high selectivity of the S-CDs to Cr(VI) ions.

In addition, sensitivity of the synthesized S-CDs towards acetone (in deionized water) was analyzed from 0.01 to 40 vol% (Fig. 4d and e). The PL band at 327 nm (λ1) decreases till it turns-off between 0.01 and 6 vol%. acetone. When the acetone concentration is over 0.08 vol%, the appearance of a new band at 411 nm (λ3) can be observed, and as acetone concentration is increased until 40 vol%, it becomes more intense (turn-on). On the other hand, the PL peak at 633 nm (λ2) is quenched as acetone concentration is increased, and after 2% vol., another PL band appears at 764 nm (λ4) and turns-on until 40 vol%. The corresponding linear range at low concentration was from 0.01 to 0.08 vol% of acetone with the estimated LOD of 0.01, 0.03, 0.02 and 0.02 vol% corresponding to the PL peaks at 327, 411, 633 and 764 nm. Stern-Volmer equation and R2 corresponding to the four PL bands are condensed in Table S1. F0/F for acetone detection in the range of 0.01–40 vol% are shown in Fig. S7a and b. Interference studies (Fig. S7c), done with respect to the relative decrease in the PL signal intensity, indicate some interfering solvents in acetone detection, such as dimethylformamide, methanol and acetonitrile. Nevertheless, the emerging bands at 492 and 759 nm allow the acetone detection in the presence of those solvents as well. Therefore, the PL intensity of four different bands can be used to detect the acetone concentration. Having several signaling reporters to determine the analyte concentration (four in the present case correspond to the four PL bands at distinct wavelengths) make the results significantly more reliable and the proposed nanoprobes very versatile.

CDs fluorescence quenching is most commonly attributed to intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and the inner filter effect (IFE) [43]. Since sulfur doped CDs provide more electron-transfer pathways [44], the PL quenching signal of S-CDs in the presence of Cr(VI) is attributed to the IFE, a process in which excitation or emission wavelengths of the S-CDs are absorbed by Cr(VI) [45,47]. Since the three absorption bands of Cr(VI) are located at 260, 360 and 440 nm, they overlap with part of the excitation band and the complete emission band of these S-CDs ( Fig. 5b). When S-CDs were excited to 260 nm (one of the absorption peaks of the heavy metal), the dual emission from S-CDs presented a linear quenching by increasing the chromium ion concentration (0–80 nM), which verifies the proposed quenching mechanism due to IFE [45]. Additionally, an XPS analysis confirms the reduction of Cr(VI) to less toxic Cr(III) in the presence of S-CDs. The high-resolution Cr 2p spectrum shows two bands corresponding to Cr 2p3/2 (576.7 eV) and Cr 2p1/2 (586.6 eV), the deconvolution of the spectrum presented in Fig. 5c displays four peaks suggesting the presence of both Cr(III) and Cr(VI) [2,3,46,48,49]. The XPS survey scan before and after chromium adsorption are included in Fig. S8a. The functional groups with oxygen, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and the sulfoxides attached onto the S-CDs surface provide more electrons which favor the Cr(VI) reduction to Cr(III) by the oxidation of some graphitic carbons [49], [50]. The Cr(VI) reduction was verified with the standard colorimetric method using diphenyl carbazide (DPC) (Fig. S8b). DPC dye is commonly used for Cr(VI) determination in water, under acidic conditions, DPC and Cr (VI) generates a violet-colored complex which can be monitored through UV-Vis spectrometry at 540 nm [51]. Here in, a continuous decrease in Cr(VI) concentration (C/C0) is considered as an additional verification (apart from XPS results) of Cr(VI) to Cr(III). Moreover, the zeta-potential after the addition of Cr(VI) solution changed from − 39.7 mV to − 33.9 mV, showing a little interaction of the heavy metal with some of the negative functional groups on the S-CDs surface by electrostatic attraction.

Moreover, acetone is a small molecule and a good electron donor, and S-CDs´ core is larger than the ligands attached on its surface, which makes the direct electron donation from acetone to the core of S-CDs possible and in turn causing the quenching of luminescence intensity. The red shift of PL as the acetone concentration increased, is possibly associated to the energy transfer from the excited state of S-CDs to the excited state of acetone [52]. In addition, solvent polarity generates different energy gap making possible the PL tunability by solvent engineering [53]. The emerging bands and the red shift of the existing bands with an increase in the acetone concentration could be related to the carbon core and surface states generated by the interaction of S-CDs and the acetone/water solution [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real tap/river water test for Cr(VI) detection and simulated and real urine, and phosphate buffer solution test for acetone detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to show the applicability of S-CDs as Cr(VI) detectors, the sensitivity tests were carried out in real water samples (tap and river water). The linear fit parameters and the corresponding calculated LODs in real water samples are included in Fig. S9. At low concentrations of metal ions, results were very similar to those obtained under laboratory conditions (Fig. S10). Variations can be attributed to other moieties present in the tap and river water. Cr(VI) detection was highly close for the three types of water in a range of 10–80 nM. Applicability of S-CDs as acetone nanoprobes was analyzed by spiking acetone in simulated urine and phosphate buffer solution. Fig. S11 shows the PL quenching with an increase in acetone concentration from 0.01 vol% to 40 vol% accompanied with the formation of the two additional bands at 411 and 764 nm. Similar tendency of F0/F is observed for the low range of 0.01–0.08 vol% of acetone by the four bands (Fig. S12). In addition, acetone was spiked in real urine collected from a baby. Fig. S13 shows the viability in detection by two emission bands located at 395 (LOD= 0.03 vol%) and 757 nm (LOD= 0.02%). With 4 vol% of acetone in real urine, the first emission band is 62 nm red shifted. When acetone concentration is increased from 4 to 8 vol%, there is a wavelength shift of 92 nm to the blue.

Taking advantage of the negatively charged functional groups in the S-CDs surface and hydroxyl groups in PVA, the S-CDs were introduced into a PVA membrane to which Cr ions could be attached/complex. These films were obtained by a simple casting method described in the experimental section. When interactions between the ions and the PVA/S-CDs films lead to complexation, rapid removal of this contaminant from the water is possible. In this work, the capacity of the PVA/S-CDs films to adsorb Cr(VI) was tested.

Part of the cross-section ( Fig. 6a and b) and surface (Fig. 6c, d and e) of the PVA/S-CDs films were visualized using FE-SEM. Images revealed a meso-porous matrix with a rough and fiber-like surface (d) which can favor the Cr(VI) retention.

Since PVA is a water-soluble polymer rich in -OH groups, it can easily form hydrogen bonds with incorporated nanomaterials and form hydrophilic composites [55]. In Fig. 6f, a clear reduction in the intensity of the stretching band vibration at 3227 cm−1 (corresponding to the -OH bond) was observed as the volume of S-CDs was increased, indicating cross-linking among PVA chains and S-CDs [56].

Fig. 6g shows the photographs of PVA films with different content of S-CDs (5%, 10% and 20% vol.) under daylight and UV (254 and 365 nm) lamp. The transparent and flexible PVA film changes to a white film under daylight after the incorporation of S-CDs and turns fluorescent under UV illumination. PVA film with 20% of S-CDs was introduced into a vessel with a 500 µM Cr(VI) solution and after three minutes the film was removed. After drying, the film changed to greenish color, likely due to the incorporation of chromium. The Cr(VI) solution with S-CDs was examined through PL signal intensity before and after the Cr(VI) exposure of the film, revealing a relatively less quenching in the remanent solution (Fig. S14) ascribable to the drop in Cr(VI) concentration by ∼ 40%. In addition, when the film was viewed under UV illumination, the blue light emitting PVA-CDs ‘films turned-off after Cr(VI) solution exposure, thus confirming the potential application of such films in waste water treatment. However, the fabricated composites have a certain degree of degradation, so the water resistance of the films needs to be improved for their usage as reusable Cr(VI) filters.

The LOD obtained herein for Cr(VI) detection is comparable to or better than the LOD of other reported CD-based fluorescent probes for Cr(VI) ( Table 1). Amongst the precursors mentioned in Table 1, there are only few that utilize pollutant waste as a carbon source. In addition, along with the reduction of Cr(VI) to less toxic ion Cr(III), the proposed S-CDs can also be used for the removal of Cr(VI) ions from water through PVA/S-CDs composite film. Apart from the fact that the detection of acetone with CDs obtained from waste carbon precursors has rarely been reported, the nanoprobe presented in this work gives us four PL bands (two in ultraviolet and two in red) ( Table 2) for the precise evaluation of the acetone content in water.

In summary, dual-emissive S-CDs were obtained through a cost-effective and simple procedure employing canola oil (new and waste) as a carbon source. Oil-based S-CDs with excitation wavelength dependent emission were used as potential optical nanoprobes for the detection of Cr(VI) and acetone in UV range (327 nm) as well as in the red region (633 nm) of the visible spectra. Although, the LODs (estimated at 327 nm) corresponding to Cr(VI) was estimated to be 20 nM in deionized water, very similar LODs were recorded in tap/river water. The low LOD towards Cr(VI) was marked by a simultaneous change in PL intensity and peak wavelength. This characteristic behavior was maintained and recognizable even in the presence of other metal contaminants. XPS analysis and colorimetric method with diphenylcarbazide revealed the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) (a less toxic form) in the presence of S-CDs. Although the estimated LOD for acetone detection in deionized water at 327 nm was 0.01 vol%, the gradual decrease in the fluorescence signal, accompanied by the appearance of an additional band at 411 nm and the same for PL band at 633 with a new appeared band at 764 nm, can be potentially applied to design a PL wavelength red-shift and intensity based sensitive dual-sensor for acetone. In addition to the reduction of Cr(VI) ions to the less toxic Cr(III) in the presence of S-CDs, luminescent S-CDs/PVA film composites were fabricated and employed to decrease Cr(VI) concentration in water by approx. 40% (from a solution 500 µM), establishing their potential application in wastewater treatment.
